event: 69 years old
female
presented to the emergency department
3-day history of high-grade fever
pleuritic right lower chest pain
cough
elevated temperature of 38.8°C
bibasilar crackles
lower extremity edema
grade 3 holosystolic apical murmur
known mitral valve regurgitation
long-term venous access port for recurrent transfusion-dependent anemia
hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL
white blood cell count, 15.1×103/µL
absolute neutrophil count 13.2×103/µL
b-type natriuretic peptide 788 pg/mL
changes consistent with congestive heart failure
previously known left bundle branch pattern
blood cultures drawn at the time of admission
positive for Corynebacterium CDC group G
gram stain positive for gram positive rods
diagnosed with bacterial IE, secondary to Corynebacterium CDC group G
mitral valve involvement
transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated
moderate mitral valve regurgitation
a thickened anterior mitral leaflet suggesting vegetations
severely elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 84 mmHg
an echocardiogram done one year prior did not have this finding of mitral valve thickening
Susceptibilities for the organism were not performed
due to a penicillin allergy
treated initially with intravenous vancomycin and clindamycin
to complete a 6-week course
chronic implanted venous access port was removed
discharged to an extended care facility to complete her antibiotic therapy
readmitted due to sudden onset shortness of breath
hypoxic
white blood cell count was elevated to 18×103/µL
repeat transthoracic echocardiography showed severe mitral valve regurgitation
large and mobile vegetation on the mitral valve
worsened from the previous echocardiogram
underwent mitral valve replacement with a 27-mm Edwards-Carpentier pericardial valve
coronary artery bypass grafting surgery was done
reverse saphenous vein graft to the distal right coronary artery
Pathology of her native mitral valve leaflet revealed endocarditis
fibrinopurulent exudate and granulation tissue
cultures were positive for diphtheroids
developed oliguric acute renal failure
respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation
vancomycin was discontinued and daptomycin therapy was started
deteriorated with worsening congestive heart failure one week out of surgery
transesophageal echocardiogram was done on day 17 of re-admission
showed severe mitral valve regurgitation
well-seeded mitral valve annular plane
concomitant echo densities were observed on the mitral valve leaflets
strongly suspicious for recurrent endocarditis of prosthetic valve
antibiotic therapy was broadened
doxycycline, aztreonam, and anidulafungin were added to her regimen
Daptomycin was continued
the porcine valve was replaced with a mechanical St. Jude’s prosthesis
an intraoperative transesophageal echocardiogram confirmed a well-functioning prosthetic mitral valve
Pathology of her bioprosthetic mitral valve showed fibrous and fibro-inflammatory tissue
cultures remained negative
developed limb ischemia
bleeding through her orifices and lines
elevation of partial thromboplastin time and prothrombin time
consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation
expired in multi-organ failure
family declined autopsy
All subsequent blood and tissue cultures performed during her admission were negative
except for the native mitral valve tissue culture positive for diphtheroids
concluded, therefore, that the initial Corynebacterium CDC group G bacteremia
was responsible for native and prosthetic valve endocarditis
review of records revealed two out of two prior blood cultures
positive for diptheroids found during an admission for pneumonia 4 months prior
had been dismissed as contaminants
not identified as a true source of infection
the potential for a line-associated bacteremia was not entertained at that time
therefore identification was not done
